policy_statement: Provide State Support and Guidance (LG5): continue/expand incentives, technical assistance, financial support, and procurement services for local governments to implement high-impact clean energy actions.
mechanism_description: The state continues and expands incentives, technical assistance, financial support, and centralized procurement services to motivate local governments to carry out clean energy projects that reduce emissions across buildings, energy supply, waste systems, and fleets.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy support (incentives/TA/financing/procurement) → ↑ deployment of clean energy actions → ↓ fossil fuel combustion / ↓ methane releases → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: State-level programs lower cost, risk, and capacity barriers for local governments to plan, procure, finance, and implement clean energy projects across buildings, energy supply, fleets, and waste operations.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Measures such as energy efficiency, electrification, renewable energy procurement, fuel switching, and improved waste management reduce fossil fuel use and/or methane generation/capture set.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂ (and potentially CH₄) emissions across multiple sectors due to accelerated deployment and higher uptake of emissions-reducing technologies and practices.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from faster and broader implementation of clean energy projects (efficiency, electrification, renewables, cleaner fleets/waste operations).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: enabling support → increased implementation of clean energy projects → reduced fossil energy use and/or methane emissions → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While procurement efficiency and capacity-building can look like “resource efficiency,” the central intended and largest climate effect is direct emissions reduction from clean energy deployment; adaptation benefits are incidental, not primary.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some supported actions could be resilience-only (e.g., backup power) or primarily cost-saving procurement improvements; however, the mechanism as defined explicitly targets “high-impact clean energy actions” that reduce emissions across sectors, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy that provides incentives/TA/financing/procurement services specifically to drive clean energy project implementation has its primary climate impact through emissions reductions, regardless of sector targeted.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: The specific mitigation subtype(s) (buildings efficiency, renewables, fleet electrification, waste methane, etc.) depend on policy-level evidence about which projects are actually funded and implemented.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If program support is used mainly for non-emissions objectives (e.g., resilience upgrades without net GHG reductions) or if procurement centralization is primarily administrative with negligible emissions impact, categorization could shift; under the stated mechanism emphasizing emissions-reducing clean energy projects, Mitigation remains the correct primary category.
key_indicators: “continuing and expanding… incentives, technical assistance, financial support, and centralized procurement services”; “motivate local governments”; “LED lighting”; “energy efficiency upgrades”; “heat pump projects”; “methane recovery for energy production”; “solar on municipal premises”; “fleet electrification”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution and associated health benefits; lower municipal energy costs/budget savings; improved energy reliability/resilience from onsite solar; workforce development for municipal staff.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a procurement-services element (could also map to procurement-requirement in other contexts), but here procurement is supportive/centralized service rather than a mandate; the dominant mechanism is incentives/financial + technical assistance enabling local action.
